event: The Return of the Prodigal Son
symbols: Father embracing the kneeling son — unconditional mercy and the joy of reconciliation
Son's ragged clothing and kneeling posture — repentance, humility, and the admission of unworthiness
The house on the right — the father's dwelling, representing God's household and the Church
Distant landscape with figures and animals — the far country from which the prodigal has returned
description: In the foreground, an elderly man in rich blue and red robes bends to embrace a kneeling younger man in simpler clothing. To the right, servants stand at the entrance of an ornate building, observing the reunion. Behind them stretches an expansive landscape with a river, trees, distant buildings, and small figures. In the far background, additional scenes of the parable appear to be depicted. The composition is bright and detailed in a miniature painting style.
theological_description: The Parable of the Prodigal Son (LUK.15.11-32) is perhaps the most beloved of all Jesus' parables, a masterpiece of divine pedagogy about the nature of God's mercy. The younger son demands his inheritance — effectively wishing his father dead — squanders it in a far country, and returns in abject poverty. The father, who has been watching and waiting, runs to embrace him before the son can finish his prepared speech of repentance (LUK.15.20-22). The father's response — the best robe, a ring, sandals, and a fatted calf — signifies complete restoration, not merely as a servant but as a beloved son. Jesus told this parable in response to the Pharisees' criticism of His association with sinners (LUK.15.1-2). The father in the parable represents God, whose love precedes and exceeds our repentance. Paul echoes this when he writes: 'While we were still sinners, Christ died for us' (ROM.5.8). The parable also foreshadows the Eucharistic feast — the great banquet of reconciliation.

scripture_refs.reason: The father sees the son from afar, runs, and embraces him — the specific scene depicted
The full parable of the Prodigal Son as told by Jesus
The context: Pharisees grumbling that Jesus receives sinners, prompting the parable
God demonstrates His love in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us — the father's preemptive mercy
God, rich in mercy, made us alive together with Christ — the restoration of the prodigal
